region_of_origin: Indian subcontinent; commonly served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Mutton Jalfrezi; closest defensible match is 'lamb dish' (FOODON_03315595). Mutton and lamb are closely related red meats and this anchor is used for ontology alignment only.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat dishes — lamb/mutton based, cooked with vegetables and spices
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ used as the closest defensible category for cooked lamb/mutton dishes. No exact Jalfrezi-specific code exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Mutton Jalfrezi is approximately 250 g, representing a main-course portion usually accompanied by rice or flatbread.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Jalfriezi (Mild Spicy) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Jalfriezi (Mild Spicy), cr_id G00010264. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from this canonical record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central lamb dish data (USDA SR Legacy #17003) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed meat-and-vegetable curry dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Jalfrezi. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous mixed meat-and-vegetable curry dishes reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). The low GI is consistent with the dish's low carbohydrate content (7.44 g/100g), high protein (14.05 g/100g), and moderate fat (11.58 g/100g), all of which attenuate postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (35 × 7.44 × 2.5) / 100 ≈ 6.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g per meal and request reduced-salt preparation, as a standard 250 g serving delivers approximately 1168 mg sodium — nearly half the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (7.44 g/100g) and high protein (14.05 g/100g) support glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton Jalfrezi is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without refined carbohydrate accompaniments such as white rice or naan; the low GI (~35) and high protein content minimise postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.8 g/100g), cholesterol (85 mg/100g), and high sodium (467 mg/100g) are relevant cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to small portions (100–150 g), choose lean mutton cuts, request oil reduction during preparation, and avoid pairing with high-sodium or high-saturated-fat accompaniments to manage cumulative lipid and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (mutton) is high in arachidonic acid; multiple nightshade vegetables present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise or avoid Mutton Jalfrezi due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of red meat and the presence of nightshade vegetables (bell pepper, tomato, green chilli), which may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Jalfrezi (Mild Spicy) is a South Asian stir-fried curry dish consisting of tender pieces of mutton (lamb) cooked with bell peppers, tomatoes, onions, green chilli, ginger, and garlic in a spiced oil-based sauce. The dish is a popular variant of the Jalfrezi style of cooking, which originated in the Indian subcontinent and is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore. The preparation involves sautéing the meat with aromatics and vegetables over high heat, producing a semi-dry curry with a mildly spiced, tangy flavour profile and a tender yet slightly chewy texture from the mutton. Per 100 g, the dish provides 192 kcal, 14.05 g protein, 11.58 g fat, and 7.44 g carbohydrate, making it a protein-rich, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. Sodium is elevated at 467 mg/100g, which is a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The low carbohydrate content and high protein make it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when served without refined carbohydrate sides. The saturated fat and cholesterol content (85 mg/100g) require moderation for cardiovascular health. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is typically halal. It is commonly consumed as a main course at lunch or dinner, paired with rice, roti, or naan in Singaporean Indian restaurant settings.
common_user_queries: How many calories in mutton jalfrezi?
Is mutton jalfrezi suitable for diabetics?
Is mutton jalfrezi high in sodium?
Can I eat mutton jalfrezi with high blood pressure?
Is lamb jalfrezi the same as mutton jalfrezi?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry (cr_id G00010264, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy lamb dish data as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions for this entry. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as no direct measurement exists for this dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Mutton Jalfrezi not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific dish preparation as of 2026. trans_fat_g reported as 0.1 g/100g based on the input health_suitability caution note referencing trans fat; this value is carried forward from the source record. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data beyond the HPB-reported fields not available in the source database for this dish. saturated_fat_g estimated at 4.8 g/100g from USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb shoulder (USDA #17003) as the closest approved-source analogue; this is an adaptation and not an exact match. All core macros are non-zero and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo. No zero-macro justification required. Aliases consistency self-check performed: all strings present in aliases_by_language (english: 'Mutton Jalfrezi (Mild Spicy)', 'Mutton Jalfriezi (Mild Spicy)', 'Lamb Jalfrezi', 'Lamb Jalfrezi (Mild)', 'Mutton Jalfrezi', 'Jalfrezi Mutton'; chinese: '羊肉炒辣椒（微辣）'; malay: 'Kambing Jalfrezi (Pedas Ringan)', 'Kambing Jalfrezi'; tamil: 'ஆட்டிறைச்சி ஜல்ஃப்ரேசி (லேசான காரம்)', 'மட்டன் ஜல்ஃப்ரேசி'; hindi: 'मटन जालफ्रेज़ी (हल्का मसालेदार)', 'मटन जालफ्रेज़ी'; romanized: 'Mutton Jalfrezi (Pedas Ringan)') verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array. Previous iteration q8 failure was caused by 'Mutton Jalfrezi (Pedas Ringan)' appearing in aliases_by_language.romanized but absent from flat aliases; this has been corrected in iteration 3.
